comments.text:
For $r=3$, if $m=|E(G)|$ and $
u(G)$ is the maximum number of \emph{edge-disjoint} copies of $K_4^3$ in $G$, then the optimal decomposition into edge-disjoint $K_4^3$'s plus single edges uses exactly $m-3
u(G)$ parts. I exhaustively checked all $2^{ inom{6}{3}}=2^{20}$ $3$-graphs on $n=6$ vertices: $\mathrm{ex}_3(6;K_4^3)=14$, and for every $G$ on $6$ vertices one has $m-3
u(G)\le 14$ (so no counterexample exists for $(r,n)=(3,6)$). Code available on request. Also $(r,n)=(7,8)$ is immediate: on $8$ vertices there are exactly $8$ $7$-edges, so $\mathrm{ex}_7(8;K_8^7)=7$, and any $7$-graph on $8$ vertices decomposes into $\le 7$ single edges if $m\le 7$, or into one $K_8^7$ if $m=8$.
